sentence: Subjective:
HPI: The patient presented with left knee pain that occurred when she was running with her 5-year-old niece, who is learning to ride a bike without training wheels.
The patient twisted her knee in a "funky way" and heard a pop, which started as a 4/10 pain but has worsened to a throbbing pain.
The pain is located on the medial aspect of the knee, more than the lateral aspect.
The patient has been limping and taking Tylenol as needed, but with limited relief.
The patient has a history of ulcers and was advised against taking NSAIDs.
Past medical history: [NOT MENTIONED]
Review of systems: [NOT MENTIONED]
Current medications: I prescribed Tylenol as needed for breakthrough pain.
Objective:
Vital signs: [NOT MENTIONED]
Physical exam: The patient had moderate range of motion in the left knee, with pain on flexion and extension.
The patient had edema and effusion in the knee.
The Lachman's test, valgus, and varus tests were negative, indicating no tears in the knee.
A x-ray of the left knee was normal, showing no fractures or bony abnormalities.
Test Results: X-ray of the left knee: normal.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has a medial collateral ligament (MCL) strain, likely sustained during a twisting injury while running with her niece.
Problem list: MCL strain; status: confirmed.
Plan:
I prescribed Tramadol 50mg twice a day for pain management.
I recommended the patient wear a knee immobilizer for the next week to prevent further movement and reduce inflammation.
I advised the patient to ice the knee 3-4 times a day for 20-30 minutes at a time.
I instructed the patient to avoid running with her niece for the next 3 weeks to allow the knee to heal.
Follow-up: I instructed the patient to follow up with me in a month to assess the progress of the knee and determine if further imaging or treatment is needed.
